era_context: The late 16th century: the Spanish Habsburg Empire in relative decline; the Ottoman Empire at its territorial zenith; the Ming Dynasty in China experiencing administrative challenges; the Reformation and Counter-Reformation reshaping European religious and political boundaries; early European colonial expansion in the Americas and Asia.

events.title: Henry IV of France converts to Catholicism
events.description: King Henry IV of France, a Protestant (Huguenot) who had led the Protestant side in the French Wars of Religion, converted to Catholicism. This conversion was politically motivated: Henry sought to end the religious civil wars and consolidate his rule over a predominantly Catholic kingdom. His conversion is famously attributed to the phrase 'Paris is worth a mass.' This event marked a turning point in the French Wars of Religion and strengthened Henry's legitimacy as king.

sources.name: Contemporary French court records and chronicles

events.certainty_note: Henry IV's conversion is well-documented. The date (July 25, 1593) is secure.

events.title: Ottoman-Safavid War: Battle of Salsu
events.description: Ottoman forces defeated Safavid forces at the Battle of Salsu in the Caucasus region. This was a significant Ottoman victory in the ongoing Ottoman-Safavid conflict. The battle demonstrated Ottoman military superiority in the region and contributed to Ottoman consolidation of control over the Caucasus and Mesopotamia.

sources.name: Ottoman and Safavid court records and chronicles

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Salsu is attested in Ottoman and Safavid sources, but the precise date (1593 or nearby years) is less certain in some sources.

events.title: Dutch capture of Coevorden from Spanish forces
events.description: Dutch forces under Maurice of Nassau captured the fortress city of Coevorden from Spanish control. This was a significant victory in the Eighty Years' War, extending Dutch control over the northern Low Countries. The capture of Coevorden strengthened the Dutch Republic's territorial position.

sources.name: Contemporary Dutch and Spanish military records
Later accounts by historians of the Eighty Years' War

events.certainty_note: The capture of Coevorden is well-documented. The date (December 1593) is secure.

events.title: English privateering and naval operations against Spain
events.description: English privateers and naval forces continued operations against Spanish shipping and colonial possessions. These operations were part of the broader Anglo-Spanish conflict and served to disrupt Spanish colonial trade and finance. The English were gradually building naval capabilities that would challenge Spanish dominance.

sources.name: English naval records and Spanish complaints

events.certainty_note: English privateering was continuous during this period, but specific events in 1593 are less precisely documented.

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Wanli Emperor's reign continues
events.description: The Wanli Emperor continued his reign over the Ming Dynasty. By 1593, the emperor was increasingly withdrawn from active governance, delegating authority to eunuchs and officials. This period saw growing factional conflicts and administrative dysfunction that would contribute to the Ming Dynasty's decline.

sources.name: Ming Dynasty records and administrative documents
Historiographic consensus on the Wanli period

events.certainty_note: The Wanli Emperor's reign is well-documented in Ming records.

events.title: Tycho Brahe's astronomical observations
events.description: Tycho Brahe continued his systematic astronomical observations at Uraniborg. By 1593, Brahe had accumulated over two decades of precise measurements. His work represented a revolution in observational astronomy and challenged the Ptolemaic model.

sources.name: Tycho Brahe's observational records and published works

events.certainty_note: Tycho Brahe's observations are well-documented. His work in 1593 is part of his continuous observational programme.

events.title: Portuguese colonial administration in the Indian Ocean
events.description: The Portuguese Empire, under the rule of Philip II of Spain (who had inherited the Portuguese throne in 1580), continued to administer its Indian Ocean colonial possessions. By 1593, Portuguese control included Goa, Macao, and various trading posts. However, Portuguese dominance was being challenged by the Dutch and English.

sources.name: Portuguese colonial records and administrative documents

events.certainty_note: Portuguese colonial administration is well-documented in colonial records.

disconfirming_evidence: Henry IV's conversion to Catholicism in 1593 has been debated by historians regarding its sincerity: some scholars argue it was purely pragmatic, while others suggest Henry had genuine religious motivations. The conversion did not immediately end the French Wars of Religion; fighting continued until the Edict of Nantes in 1598. Additionally, the Battle of Salsu's date and significance have been debated by Ottoman and Persian historians, with some sources placing it in different years or questioning its strategic importance.
historiographic_note: The year 1593 is well-documented in European sources, particularly for France, Spain, England, and the Dutch Republic. Ottoman and Safavid sources are available but less frequently consulted in Western historiography. Ming Dynasty records exist but are less frequently examined for this specific year. The bias toward European and Atlantic history is pronounced; events in sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas beyond Spanish and English territories, and Oceania are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Henry IV's conversion in 1593 led to his coronation at Chartres in 1594
Dutch capture of Coevorden was part of the broader Eighty Years' War
Ottoman-Safavid conflict continued across multiple years
